**Paris** is a city in and the [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") of [Lamar County, Texas](/wiki/Lamar_County,_Texas "Lamar County, Texas"), United States. Located in [Northeast Texas](/wiki/Northeast_Texas "Northeast Texas") at the western edge of the [Piney Woods](/wiki/Piney_Woods "Piney Woods"), the population of the city was 24,171 in 2020.[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paris,_Texas&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Present-day Lamar County was part of [Red River County](/wiki/Red_River_County,_Texas "Red River County, Texas") during the [Republic of Texas](/wiki/Republic_of_Texas "Republic of Texas"). By 1840, population growth necessitated the organization of a new county. George Washington Wright, who had served in the Third [Congress of the Republic of Texas](/wiki/Congress_of_the_Republic_of_Texas "Congress of the Republic of Texas") as a representative from Red River County, was a major proponent of the new county. The Fifth Congress established the new county on December 17, 1840, and named it after [Mirabeau B. Lamar](/wiki/Mirabeau_B._Lamar "Mirabeau B. Lamar"),[[5]](#cite_note-5) who was the first vice president and the second president of the Republic of Texas.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/Old_map-Paris-1885.jpg/250px-Old_map-Paris-1885.jpg)](/wiki/File:Old_map-Paris-1885.jpg)

Paris, Texas in 1885

Lamar County was one of the 18 Texas counties that voted against secession on February 23, 1861.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

In 1877, 1896, and 1916, major fires in the city forced considerable rebuilding. The 1916 fire destroyed almost half the town and caused an estimated $11 million in property damage. The fire ruined most of the central business district and swept through a residential area. The burned structures included the Federal Building and Post Office, the Lamar County Courthouse and Jail, City Hall, most commercial buildings, and several churches.[[7]](#cite_note-1916Fire-7)

In 1893, black teenager [Henry Smith](/wiki/Henry_Smith_(lynching_victim) "Henry Smith (lynching victim)") was accused of murder, tortured, and then burned to death on a scaffold in front of thousands of spectators in Paris.[[8]](#cite_note-nyt.lynch-8) In 1920, [two black brothers from the Arthur family](/wiki/Lynching_of_Irving_and_Herman_Arthur "Lynching of Irving and Herman Arthur") were tied to a flagpole and burned to death at the Paris fairgrounds. The city has prominent memorials to the [Confederacy](/wiki/Confederate_States_of_America "Confederate States of America").[[8]](#cite_note-nyt.lynch-8)

In 1943, the [U.S. Supreme Court](/wiki/U.S._Supreme_Court "U.S. Supreme Court") in *[Largent v. Texas](/wiki/Largent_v._Texas "Largent v. Texas")* struck down a Paris ordinance that prohibited a person from selling or distributing religious publications without first obtaining a city-issued permit. The court ruled that the ordinance abridged freedom of religion, freedom of speech, and freedom of the press in violation of the [Fourteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution](/wiki/Fourteenth_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Fourteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution").[[9]](#cite_note-9)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Paristrains.jpg/250px-Paristrains.jpg)](/wiki/File:Paristrains.jpg)

Historic Paris train station

Paris is a former railroad center. The [Texas and Pacific](/wiki/Texas_and_Pacific "Texas and Pacific") reached town in 1876; the [Gulf, Colorado and Santa Fe Railway](/wiki/Gulf,_Colorado_and_Santa_Fe_Railway "Gulf, Colorado and Santa Fe Railway") (later merged into the Atchison, Topeka and Santa Fe Railway) and the Frisco in 1887; the Texas Midland Railroad (later [Southern Pacific](/wiki/Southern_Pacific_Railroad "Southern Pacific Railroad")) in 1894; and the Paris and Mount Pleasant (Pa-Ma Line) in 1910. Paris Union Station, built 1912, served Frisco, Santa Fe, and Texas Midland passenger trains until 1956. Today, the station is used by the Lamar County Chamber of Commerce and serves as the research library for the Lamar County Genealogical Society.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Following a tradition of American cities named "Paris" (named after [France's capital](/wiki/Paris,_France "Paris, France")), the city commissioned a 65-foot-tall (20 m) [replica](/wiki/Eiffel_Tower_(Paris,_Texas) "Eiffel Tower (Paris, Texas)") of the [Eiffel Tower](/wiki/Eiffel_Tower "Eiffel Tower") in 1993 and installed it on site of the Love Civic Center, southeast of the town square. In 1998, presumably as a response to the 1993 construction of a 60-foot-tall (18 m) [tower](/wiki/Eiffel_Tower_(Paris,_Tennessee) "Eiffel Tower (Paris, Tennessee)") in [Paris, Tennessee](/wiki/Paris,_Tennessee "Paris, Tennessee"), the city placed a giant red cowboy hat atop its tower. The current Eiffel Tower replica is at least the second one; an earlier replica constructed of wood was destroyed by a [tornado](/wiki/Tornado "Tornado").

### Race relations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paris,_Texas&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Race relations")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Henry-smith-2-1-1893-paris-tx-2.jpg/250px-Henry-smith-2-1-1893-paris-tx-2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Henry-smith-2-1-1893-paris-tx-2.jpg)

Lynching of Henry Smith, Paris Fairgrounds, 1893

Paris has had a [White](/wiki/Non-Hispanic_whites "Non-Hispanic whites") majority with a significant black minority for most of its history.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The city is deeply segregated[[11]](#cite_note-Witt-11) and race relations in Paris have a bloody history[[12]](#cite_note-Witt2-12) and are deeply polarized,[[12]](#cite_note-Witt2-12) turbulent,[[13]](#cite_note-Kovach-13) and sometimes explosive.[[13]](#cite_note-Kovach-13)

In the late-19th and early-20th centuries, several [lynchings](/wiki/Lynching_in_the_United_States "Lynching in the United States") were staged at the Paris Fairgrounds as public spectacles, with crowds of White spectators cheering as the African-American victims were tortured and murdered.[[11]](#cite_note-Witt-11)[[12]](#cite_note-Witt2-12) A black teenager named [Henry Smith](/wiki/Henry_Smith_(lynching_victim) "Henry Smith (lynching victim)") was lynched in 1893. His murder was the first lynching in US history that was captured in photographs sold as postcards and other trinkets commemorating the killing.[[14]](#cite_note-Minutaglio-14) Journalist [Ida B. Wells](/wiki/Ida_B._Wells "Ida B. Wells") said of the incident, "Never in the history of civilization has any Christian people stooped to such shocking brutality and indescribable barbarism as that which characterized the people of Paris, Texas."[[14]](#cite_note-Minutaglio-14)

On July 7, 1920 [Irving and Herman Arthur](/wiki/Lynching_of_Irving_and_Herman_Arthur "Lynching of Irving and Herman Arthur") were burned alive at the fairgrounds before a crowd of 3,000,[[15]](#cite_note-15) their charred corpses then being dragged by a convoy of White people through Paris's African-American neighborhood as a warning to the black community.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/1cherryandblackwell.jpg/250px-1cherryandblackwell.jpg)](/wiki/File:1cherryandblackwell.jpg)

Local resident and activist [Brenda Cherry](/wiki/Brenda_Cherry "Brenda Cherry") speaking at the rally for Brandon McClelland, 2009

In 2008, an African-American man, [Brandon McClelland](/wiki/Death_of_Brandon_McClelland "Death of Brandon McClelland"), was run over and dragged to death under a vehicle. Two White men were arrested, but the prosecutor cited lack of evidence and declined to press charges, and no serious subsequent attempt to find other perpetrators was made. This caused unrest in the Paris African-American community. Following this incident, an attempt by the [United States Department of Justice](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Justice "United States Department of Justice") Community Relations Service to initiate a dialogue between the races in the town[[17]](#cite_note-Abshire-17) ended in failure when African-American complaints were mostly met by silent glares from white community members.[[12]](#cite_note-Witt2-12)

A 2009 protest rally over the case led to [Texas State Police](/wiki/List_of_law_enforcement_agencies_in_Texas "List of law enforcement agencies in Texas") intervention to prevent groups shouting "white power!" and "black power!" from coming to blows.[[18]](#cite_note-Carlton-18) In response to the incident, civil rights activist [Brenda Cherry](/wiki/Brenda_Cherry "Brenda Cherry") said "I think we are probably stuck in 1930 right about now".[[19]](#cite_note-McKinley-19)
In 2007, a 14-year-old African-American girl was sentenced by a local judge to up to seven years in a youth prison for shoving a hall monitor at Paris High School. Three months earlier, the same judge had sentenced a 14-year-old White girl to probation for arson. This sentencing disparity occasioned nationwide controversy[[20]](#cite_note-Witt4-20) and the African-American girl was released after serving one year on orders of a special conservator appointed by the State of Texas to investigate problems with the state's juvenile-justice practices.[[20]](#cite_note-Witt4-20)

In 2009, some African-American workers at the [Turner Industries Group](/w/index.php?title=Turner_Industries_Group&action=edit&redlink=1 "Turner Industries Group (page does not exist)") facility in the city claimed that hangman's nooses, Confederate flags, and racist graffiti were common occurrences due to a select group of employees on the site.[[21]](#cite_note-Witt3-21) At the same time, the [United States Department of Education](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Education "United States Department of Education") was conducting an investigation into allegations that African-American students in Paris's schools are disciplined more harshly than White students for similar offenses.[[20]](#cite_note-Witt4-20)

In 2015, the United States [Equal Employment Opportunity Commission](/wiki/Equal_Employment_Opportunity_Commission "Equal Employment Opportunity Commission") ruled after an investigation that African-American workers at the [Sara Lee Corporation](/wiki/Sara_Lee_Corporation "Sara Lee Corporation") plant in Paris (closed in 2011)[[22]](#cite_note-Cancino-22) were deliberately exposed disproportionately to asbestos, black mold, and other toxins, and also were targets of racial slurs and racist graffiti.[[23]](#cite_note-NBC5-23)

Some Paris residents downplay the extent to which the town has a race-relations problem.[[11]](#cite_note-Witt-11)[[18]](#cite_note-Carlton-18) Judge M. C. Superville commented, "I do not believe there is systematic racial discrimination in Lamar County. I do believe there is a misperception that that is going on".[[19]](#cite_note-McKinley-19)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paris,_Texas&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Geography")]

According to the [U.S. Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), the city has a total area of 44.4 square miles (115 km2), of which 1.7 square miles (4.4 km2) (3.74%) are covered by water.

Paris is located in "[Tornado Alley](/wiki/Tornado_Alley "Tornado Alley")", an area largely centered in the middle of the United States in which tornadoes occur frequently because of weather patterns and geography. Paris is in [USDA plant hardiness zone](/wiki/USDA_plant_hardiness_zone "USDA plant hardiness zone") 8a for winter temperatures. This is cooler than its southern neighbor [Dallas](/wiki/Dallas "Dallas"), and while similar to [Atlanta, Georgia](/wiki/Atlanta,_Georgia "Atlanta, Georgia"), it has warmer summertime temperatures. Summertime average highs reach 94 and 95 °F (34 and 35 °C) in July and August, with associated lows of 72 and 71 °F (22 and 22 °C). Winter temperatures drop to an average high of 51 °F (11 °C) and low of 30 °F (−1 °C) in January. The highest temperature on record was 115 °F (46 °C), set in August 1936, and the record low was −5 °F (−21 °C), set in 1930. Average precipitation is 47.82 in (1,215 mm). Snow is not unusual, but is by no means predictable, and years can pass with no snowfall at all.

On April 2, 1982, [Paris was hit](/wiki/Tornado_outbreak_of_April_2%E2%80%933,_1982#Paris–Reno–Blossom,_Texas "Tornado outbreak of April 2–3, 1982") by an [F4](/wiki/Fujita_scale#Parameters "Fujita scale") [tornado](/wiki/Tornado "Tornado") that destroyed more than 1,500 homes, and left 10 people dead, 170 injured, and 3,000 homeless. The damage toll from this tornado was estimated at US$50 million in 1982.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paris,_Texas&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Climate")]

According to the [Köppen Climate Classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_Climate_Classification "Köppen Climate Classification"), Paris has a [humid subtropical climate](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate"), a*Cfa* on climate maps. The hottest temperature recorded in Paris was 115 °F (46.1 °C) in August 1936, while the coldest temperature recorded was −5 °F (−20.6 °C) in January 1930 although unofficially it was −12 °F (−24.4 °C) in [February 1899](/wiki/Great_Blizzard_of_1899 "Great Blizzard of 1899").[[25]](#cite_note-NOWData-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)

From an [1880 United States census](/wiki/1880_United_States_census "1880 United States census") population of 3,980, the population of the city of Paris increased to 25,898 at the [2000 census](/wiki/2000_United_States_census "2000 United States census");[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4). However, by 2020 the population had declined to 24,171.[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4)

### 2020 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paris,_Texas&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2020 census")]

As of the 2020 census, Paris had a population of 24,476 and a median age of 38.7 years.[[30]](#cite_note-Census2020DP-30) Twenty-three point seven percent of residents were under the age of 18, and 19.5% were 65 years of age or older. For every 100 females there were 87.4 males, and for every 100 females age 18 and over there were 83.5 males age 18 and over.[[30]](#cite_note-Census2020DP-30)

95.0% of residents lived in urban areas, while 5.0% lived in rural areas.[[31]](#cite_note-Census2020DHC-31)

As of the 2020 census, there were 10,185 households in Paris, of which 28.8% had children under the age of 18 living in them. Of all households, 34.3% were married-couple households, 20.0% were households with a male householder and no spouse or partner present, and 39.2% were households with a female householder and no spouse or partner present. About 35.1% of all households were made up of individuals and 16.5% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older.[[30]](#cite_note-Census2020DP-30)

As of the 2020 census, there were 11,569 housing units, of which 12.0% were vacant. The homeowner vacancy rate was 2.2% and the rental vacancy rate was 10.1%.[[30]](#cite_note-Census2020DP-30)

### 2010 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paris,_Texas&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2010 census")]

In 2010, 25,171 people, 10,306 households, and 6,426 families resided in the city.[[33]](#cite_note-GR2-33) The population density was 588.1 people per square mile (227.1 people/km2). The 11,883 housing units averaged 277.6 per square mile (107.2/km2). The average household size was 2.38 and the average family size was 3.01. In the city, the population was distributed as 25.0% under 18, 10.6% from 18 to 24, 24.1% from 25 to 44, 23.8% from 45 to 64, and 16.6% who were 65 or older. The median age was 37.1 years. For every 100 females, there were 87.3 males. For every 100 females 18 and over, there were 82.9 males.

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paris,_Texas&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Economy")]

In the past, Paris was a major cotton exchange, and the county was developed as cotton plantations. While cotton is still farmed on the lands around Paris, it is no longer a major part of the economy.

Paris's one major hospital had two campuses: Paris Regional Medical Center South (formerly St. Joseph's Hospital) and Paris Regional Medical Center North (formerly McCuistion Regional Medical Center). It serves as the center of healthcare for much of Northeast Texas and Southeast Oklahoma. Both campuses were operated jointly under the name of the Paris Regional Medical Center, a division of Essent Healthcare. Paris Regional Medical Center South Campus has closed and only the North Campus remains open. The health network is one of the largest employers in the Paris area.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

Outside of healthcare, the largest employers are [Kimberly-Clark](/wiki/Kimberly-Clark "Kimberly-Clark") and [Campbell Soup](/wiki/Campbell_Soup "Campbell Soup").

## Arts and culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paris,_Texas&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Arts and culture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Culbertson_fountain_2010.jpg/250px-Culbertson_fountain_2010.jpg)](/wiki/File:Culbertson_fountain_2010.jpg)

The Culbertson Fountain

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Paris_Texas_Eiffel.jpg/250px-Paris_Texas_Eiffel.jpg)](/wiki/File:Paris_Texas_Eiffel.jpg)

The 65-foot (20 m) tall Paris Eiffel Tower with the red cowboy hat at its apex

The city is home to several late-19th to mid-20th-century stately homes. Among these is the Rufus Fenner Scott Mansion, designed by German architect J.L. Wees and constructed in 1910. The structure is solid concrete and steel with four floors. Rufus Scott was a prominent businessman known for shipping, imports, and banking. He was well known by local farmers, who bought aging transport mules from him. The Scott Mansion narrowly survived the fire of 1916. After the fire, Scott brought the architect Wees back to Paris to redesign the historic downtown area.[[38]](#cite_note-ScottMansion-38)

This is the site of a noted 12-foot (3.7 m) tall "Jesus with cowboy boots" statue and grave marker of [Willet Babcock](/wiki/Willet_Babcock "Willet Babcock"), as well as the resting place of banker/philanthropist [William J. McDonald](/wiki/William_Johnson_McDonald "William Johnson McDonald"), Confederate General/U.S. Senator [Sam Bell Maxey](/wiki/Samuel_Bell_Maxey "Samuel Bell Maxey"), rancher Pitts Chisum, and cotton magnate John J. Culbertson. Pitts Chisum's more famous brother, [John Chisum](/wiki/John_Chisum "John Chisum"), is also buried in the city.
* [Sam Bell Maxey House](/wiki/Sam_Bell_Maxey_House "Sam Bell Maxey House") – Maxey was a Confederate general and two-time US senator.

## Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paris,_Texas&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Government")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/Paris_July_2015_25_%28City_Hall%29.jpg/250px-Paris_July_2015_25_%28City_Hall%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Paris_July_2015_25_(City_Hall).jpg)

City Hall in July 2015

Paris is governed by a [city council](/wiki/City_council "City council") as specified in the city's charter adopted in 1948.

Paris is represented in the [Texas Senate](/wiki/Texas_Senate "Texas Senate") by Republican [Bryan Hughes](/wiki/Bryan_Hughes_(politician) "Bryan Hughes (politician)"), District 1, and in the [Texas House of Representatives](/wiki/Texas_House_of_Representatives "Texas House of Representatives") by Republican [Gary VanDeaver](/wiki/Gary_VanDeaver "Gary VanDeaver"), District 1.

The [Texas Department of Criminal Justice](/wiki/Texas_Department_of_Criminal_Justice "Texas Department of Criminal Justice") operates the Paris District Parole Office[[39]](#cite_note-TDCR-39)

At the federal level, the two U.S. senators from Texas are Republicans [John Cornyn](/wiki/John_Cornyn "John Cornyn") and [Ted Cruz](/wiki/Ted_Cruz "Ted Cruz"). Paris is part of [Texas's 4th congressional district](/wiki/Texas%27s_4th_congressional_district "Texas's 4th congressional district"), represented by Republican [Pat Fallon](/wiki/Pat_Fallon "Pat Fallon").

The [United States Postal Service](/wiki/United_States_Postal_Service "United States Postal Service") operates the Paris Post Office.[[40]](#cite_note-WhitePages-40)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paris,_Texas&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Paris_July_2015_20_%28Paris_Public_Library%29.jpg/250px-Paris_July_2015_20_%28Paris_Public_Library%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Paris_July_2015_20_(Paris_Public_Library).jpg)

Paris Public Library in July 2015

Elementary and secondary education is split among these [school districts](/wiki/School_district "School district"):[[41]](#cite_note-LamarCountySDMap2020-41)

* [Paris Independent School District](/wiki/Paris_Independent_School_District "Paris Independent School District")
* [North Lamar Independent School District](/wiki/North_Lamar_Independent_School_District "North Lamar Independent School District")
* [Chisum Independent School District](/wiki/Chisum_Independent_School_District "Chisum Independent School District")

In addition, [Paris Junior College](/wiki/Paris_Junior_College "Paris Junior College") provides postsecondary education. The Texas Education Code specifies that all of the Paris city limits is in the community college's service area. The TEC additionally specifies that areas in Paris ISD and all of Lamar County are in the college's service area.[[42]](#cite_note-42) It hosts the Texas Institute of Jewelry Technology, a well-respected school[*[according to whom?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] of gemology, horology, and jewelry. The Industrial Technology Division offers programs in air conditioning technology, refrigeration technology, agricultural technology, drafting and computer-aided design, electronics, electromechanical technology, and welding technology.

[East Texas A&M University](/wiki/East_Texas_A%26M_University "East Texas A&M University"), a major university of over 12,000 students, is located in the neighboring city of [Commerce](/wiki/Commerce,_Texas "Commerce, Texas"), 40 miles (64 km) southwest of Paris.

The Paris Public Library serves Paris, as does the Lamar County Genealogical Society Library.[[43]](#cite_note-ParisGov1-43)

According to the [Texas Transportation Commission](/wiki/Texas_Transportation_Commission "Texas Transportation Commission"), Paris is the second-largest city in Texas without a four-lane divided highway connecting to an [interstate highway](/wiki/Interstate_highway "Interstate highway") within the state.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] However, those traveling north of the city can go into the Midwest on a four-lane thoroughfare via US 271 across the Red River into Oklahoma, and then the [Indian Nation Turnpike](/wiki/Indian_Nation_Turnpike "Indian Nation Turnpike") from Hugo to Interstate 40 at Henryetta, which in turn continues as a free four-lane highway via US 75 to [Tulsa](/wiki/Tulsa "Tulsa").

#### Mass transit

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paris,_Texas&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Mass transit")]

For public transit, Paris is served by the Ark-Tex Council of Governments Rural Transit District (TRAX). Local, fixed-route bus service runs hourly on weekdays between 6:30 am and 6:30 pm.[[44]](#cite_note-44) Dubbed the "Paris Metro", *Texas Monthly* has cited the town as a model for rural transport.[[45]](#cite_note-45) There is no intercity transit available in Paris. However, intercity bus routes can be accessed in nearby Mount Pleasant and Sulphur Springs. These services are operated by [Greyhound](/wiki/Greyhound_Lines "Greyhound Lines")[[46]](#cite_note-46) and [Trailways](/wiki/Trailways_Transportation_System "Trailways Transportation System").

#### Rail

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paris,_Texas&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Rail")]

Paris is served by a [branch line](/wiki/Branch_line "Branch line") of the [Kiamichi Railroad](/wiki/Kiamichi_Railroad "Kiamichi Railroad") leading to [Hugo, Oklahoma](/wiki/Hugo,_Oklahoma "Hugo, Oklahoma"), and is the eastern terminus of a [Dallas, Garland and Northeastern Railroad](/wiki/Dallas,_Garland_and_Northeastern_Railroad "Dallas, Garland and Northeastern Railroad") line to [Sherman, Texas](/wiki/Sherman,_Texas "Sherman, Texas").[[47]](#cite_note-47) As of 2024, the rail lines are used for freight haulage only; the city is not served by [Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak").[[48]](#cite_note-48)

#### Air

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paris,_Texas&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Air")]

[Cox Field](/wiki/Cox_Field "Cox Field") provides [general aviation](/wiki/General_aviation "General aviation") services, and is [categorized](/wiki/FAA_airport_categories "FAA airport categories") by the [Federal Aviation Administration](/wiki/Federal_Aviation_Administration "Federal Aviation Administration") as a *Regional* airport.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

#### Trails

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paris,_Texas&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Trails")]

Paris is located on the [Northeast Texas Trail](/wiki/Northeast_Texas_Trail "Northeast Texas Trail") (NETT), a 130 mi (210 km) hike-and-bike trail from [Farmersville, Texas](/wiki/Farmersville,_Texas "Farmersville, Texas"), to [New Boston, Texas](/wiki/New_Boston,_Texas "New Boston, Texas"), which follows a disused railroad right-of-way [railbanked](/wiki/Railbanked "Railbanked") by the [Union Pacific Railroad](/wiki/Union_Pacific_Railroad "Union Pacific Railroad") and [Chaparral Railroad](/w/index.php?title=Chaparral_Railroad&action=edit&redlink=1 "Chaparral Railroad (page does not exist)") in the 1990s.[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[51]](#cite_note-51)
